metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the concrete stuck-state alarm threshold value to S5c (e.g., 'fire if time-in-phase > N minutes; N should exceed the S2 Verified-await bound of 30 min, e.g., 45 min'), co-located with the alarm AC and the unit-check requirement.
findings.finding: The stuck-state alarm in S5c fires 'when a `wipChain` sits in any non-`idle` phase past a threshold' but the threshold value is never stated. Unlike the heartbeat contract (which explicitly states ≤15 s freshness and >90 s lander_down), the time-in-phase threshold has no specified default, no reference to a related timeout (e.g., the S2 30-min Verified-await bound), and no unit-check anchor.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Stuck-state alarm (high visibility)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete detection protocol for the `lander_down`/handback feeder: either specify a `AUTOLANDER_DOWN` stdout marker the loop emits when it detects heartbeat staleness, or specify that `observability.sh` polls the status endpoint for `heartbeat_age_s > 90` and increments the counter — and add this to the S5c error-metric AC.
findings.finding: The third feeder of the `autolander_errors` metric in S5c is 'each `lander_down`/handback event.' The `lander_down` concept originates in S4 (the `land` command reads `heartbeat_age_s` from the HTTP status endpoint). `observability.sh` is a host-side bash script that has no access to S4's client logic. No protocol is defined for how `observability.sh` detects or counts a `lander_down`/handback event — no stdout marker, no log pattern, no state file, and no alternative detection mechanism.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' — third feeder

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete end-state proof for the 'service logs, not session_log' guarantee — e.g., a pytest/smoke assertion that running the loop under test emits no `session_log` writes, or a compose `logging.driver` config that routes stdout/stderr to the service log and a CI grep that confirms no `session_log` call-site remains in `infra/autolander/`.
findings.finding: The title's headline deliverable — 'service logs, not session_log' — lacks a concrete, end-state-observable proof in the plan body. No log-routing config (compose log-driver, stdout redirect) is specified, no test asserts the absence of session_log writes, and no CI/smoke check is called out. This means the team cannot objectively verify the headline guarantee at deploy time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet: 'The service NEVER writes session_log'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c or S5d, add an AC line such as: 'A CI lint/grep step asserts no `session_log` import or call exists in `infra/autolander/`; the compose service definition sets `logging.driver: json-file` (or the site standard) to route container stdout/stderr to service logs.' Alternatively, add a unit test that monkeypatches `session_log` and asserts it is never called during a loop iteration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the status endpoint's binding (loopback `127.0.0.1:<port>` or explicit no-auth rationale), add the corresponding nginx `location` block to `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`, and state how human/admin principals authenticate to (or are blocked from) the endpoint.
findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c) is a new network-reachable service whose human/admin authentication contract is not specified. The plan says 'bound so agents can reach it' and 'Read-only, non-secret' but never states the binding address (loopback vs. all-interfaces), whether/how it is proxied through the host nginx (the existing nginx config has no location block for it), or what human/admin auth mechanism (OIDC/HTTP-auth/gateway) applies. Without an explicit 'loopback-only' or 'behind the existing TLS+nginx gateway' declaration — and a corresponding nginx proxy rule — this endpoint's access posture is ambiguous; if it binds on 0.0.0.0 it is directly internet-reachable with no auth.
findings.location: AC: Observability + status endpoint (S5c) / S5c child ticket: 'Read-only, non-secret; bound so agents can reach it'
findings.suggested_fix: Bind the endpoint to `127.0.0.1:<AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT>` in docker-compose.yml (matching the review-bot pattern), add a `location /autolander/` proxy block in rebar.conf.template, and document that the endpoint is only reachable via the existing nginx TLS gateway — which enforces the same network access control as Gerrit and the review-bot. Then no additional auth layer is needed, and the rationale ('behind the existing TLS nginx gateway, single-tenant VPC') should be written into the plan as the explicit justification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the status endpoint's access class (e.g., 'internal-only, bound to 127.0.0.1 or Docker internal network, no auth required' OR 'host-network, unauthenticated-by-design, restricted to VPC security group') in S5c's AC — ambiguity about the binding address and reachability is itself the gap.
findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c) does not declare its access posture — whether it is public/unauthenticated, internal-network-only, or authenticated — making its trust boundary undeclared. "Bound so agents can reach it" is ambiguous about binding address and network reachability.
findings.location: S5c AC – 'Read-only status endpoint' / child ticket 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single line to S5c's AC: 'The endpoint MUST bind to 127.0.0.1 (or the Docker internal network) only; it is not published on the host interface and is not reachable from outside the Gerrit box.' If cross-host reachability is required (e.g., for a monitoring agent on another host), state that explicitly and confirm the VPC security group restricts access.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` marker's required JSON fields (e.g., `{"AUTOLANDER_ERROR": true, "change_id": ..., "phase": ..., "error": ...}`) and the exact grep token `observability.sh` will use to count them, co-located with the S5c error-metric AC.
findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` structured stdout marker (feeder 1 of the autolander_errors metric in S5c) is referenced by name but its JSON shape and the grep/parse pattern that `observability.sh` would use to count it are never defined. The plan cites `VOTER_ERROR`/`G2P` as prior art, but those patterns differ: `VOTER_ERROR` is a structured JSON keyword greppable by `grep -cE 'VOTER_ERROR'` against journald output. The exact field set of `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` (error type, change_id, stack_id, phase, etc.) is absent, yet `observability.sh` must be edited to count it.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' — first feeder; child task description 'the AUTOLANDER_ERROR structured stdout marker'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a marker spec to S5c: 'The loop emits `AUTOLANDER_ERROR {"change_id":"...","phase":"...","error":"..."}` to stdout; `observability.sh` counts it via `grep -cE AUTOLANDER_ERROR` against the autolander container journal, matching the VOTER_ERROR pattern in section 4.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in S5c's AC whether loop.py emits a structured stdout marker (ingested by observability.sh's PutMetricData feeder) OR calls CloudWatch SDK directly (making observability.sh's feeder for this metric redundant). Only one path should publish each datapoint.
findings.finding: `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` has an ambiguous emission path: S5c's loop.py edit 'emits' the metric AND S5c's observability.sh has a 'PutMetricData feeder' for it. It is unspecified whether loop.py calls CloudWatch SDK directly, writes a structured stdout marker that observability.sh ingests, or both — creating a risk of double-counting or undefined split of responsibility.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) — Time-to-land metric AC + Files section
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the error-metric pattern: loop.py writes a structured stdout marker (e.g. `AUTOLANDER_LAND_SECONDS=<value>`), and observability.sh's PutMetricData feeder ingests it. Remove any direct CloudWatch SDK call for this metric from loop.py, or remove the observability.sh feeder for it — whichever is chosen, document it explicitly in S5c's AC.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `stop_grace_period` to the `autolander` service in `docker-compose.yml` set to at least the CI-wait timeout (the maximum time a drain could need to finish the in-flight wipChain), so the SIGTERM drain can actually run to completion before Docker sends SIGKILL.
findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain mechanism in S5d is effectively inoperative in most real-world shutdown scenarios because the plan does not specify setting `stop_grace_period` in docker-compose.yml. Docker Compose's default stop_grace_period is 10 seconds; after that Docker sends SIGKILL. Since the in-flight wipChain may be awaiting a fresh Verified +1 (which takes the full CI run time — potentially many minutes), the 10-second window will almost always expire before the drain completes, and the bot will always fall through to writing the recovery record and exiting early. The recovery record correctly handles correctness, but the plan states drain-to-completion as the preferred path and names it the 'RELIABILITY FALLBACK backbone' — the fallback is always being taken silently without the drain ever completing.
findings.location: S5d AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery' / infra/compose/docker-compose.yml (not yet authored)
findings.suggested_fix: In the autolander service definition in infra/compose/docker-compose.yml, add `stop_grace_period: <CI_WAIT_TIMEOUT>s` (e.g. `stop_grace_period: 600s` if the CI timeout is 10 minutes). The value should match or exceed the CI-wait timeout used by S2's await-Verified loop so a shutdown during the awaiting_verified phase can drain cleanly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical `phase` enum value set (e.g., `idle | rebasing | awaiting_verified | submitting | paused | mid-handback`) in one authoritative location (Design context or S5c/S5d shared contract) and reference it from all consuming ACs.
findings.finding: The `phase` field of `wipChain`, referenced by the S5c status endpoint, the stuck-state alarm ('non-idle phase'), the S5d emergency-stop ('transitions to `paused`'), and the S5d crash-recovery test ('kills at each phase: rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, mid-handback'), has no single canonical enum definition anywhere in S5 or its children. Values are scattered: S5d crash-test implies {rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, mid-handback}; S5d emergency-stop adds `paused`; S5c implies `idle`. The set is inconsistent and never enumerated in one authoritative place.
findings.location: S5c AC (status endpoint, stuck-state alarm); S5d AC (emergency-stop, crash-recovery test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Phase vocabulary (design-fixed)' subsection to the Design context block listing all valid phase strings, then cross-reference it from S5c's status-endpoint AC, S5c's stuck-state alarm AC, S5d's emergency-stop AC, and S5d's crash-recovery test AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the heartbeat_age_s sentinel value when the heartbeat file is absent (e.g. return the time since container start, or a large sentinel like 999999, or null with lander_down treating null as down), and add this to S5c's status endpoint AC.
findings.finding: No child specifies the behavior of the status endpoint (S5c) when the heartbeat file has not yet been written (e.g. on first startup before S5d's loop.py writes the first heartbeat, or when S5c is deployed before S5d). `land`'s `lander_down` binds to `heartbeat_age_s` — a missing or null value would break the consumer.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) — Read-only status endpoint AC + child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) — Heartbeat AC
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c's endpoint AC, add: 'If the heartbeat file does not exist, heartbeat_age_s is returned as the time since container start (treating it as effectively stale). lander_down treats any value > 90 s or null as down.' In S5d's heartbeat AC, add: 'Write the heartbeat file immediately on startup (before the first poll completes) to minimize the window of heartbeat_age_s ambiguity.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why `willfarrell/autoheal` is chosen over (a) a host-side `docker events` watchdog and (b) Docker Engine's native health-aware restart, so the architectural choice is justified rather than assumed.
findings.finding: The plan adopts `willfarrell/autoheal` as the sole hang-recovery mechanism without considering established alternatives: Docker Compose v2 added first-class restart-on-unhealthy semantics (via `restart: unless-stopped` + HEALTHCHECK, and health-based dependency restart in Swarm mode), and a lightweight host-side `docker events --filter event=health_status=unhealthy` watchdog achieves the same recovery with zero new image dependencies. The plan names 'restart: unless-stopped only fires on process EXIT' as the sole justification for the sidecar but does not evaluate whether the native compose restart policy (which DOES respond to HEALTHCHECK state on recent Docker Engine versions) would suffice, or whether a host-side watcher is simpler.
findings.location: S5a AC — 'autoheal sidecar'; S5d AC — 'willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (S5a) restarts the autoheal=true-labeled container on unhealthy'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph rationale in S5a or a referenced ADR: if the native Docker restart policy (`restart: unless-stopped` does NOT react to HEALTHCHECK on plain Compose without Swarm — confirm this for the exact Docker Engine version on the box), document that finding; if a host-side watchdog is rejected, state why. This makes the tradeoff explicit and reviewable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'scoped privilege' claim for the Docker socket `:ro` mount: acknowledge that `:ro` on a Unix domain socket does NOT restrict Docker API operations, and explicitly note the full-API blast radius of the autoheal sidecar in the deploy review surface.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that mounting `/var/run/docker.sock:ro` for the autoheal sidecar is a 'scoped privilege' that restricts its Docker API access. This capability claim is incorrect: for a Unix domain socket, `:ro` prevents new filesystem entries in the socket's parent directory but does NOT restrict bidirectional I/O on the socket itself — autoheal therefore retains full Docker API access (exec, image pull, container start/stop, volume manipulation) identical to a `:rw` mount. The plan's 'scoped privilege' framing is an unverified assertion that will mislead deploy reviewers about the sidecar's actual blast radius.
findings.location: S5a AC — 'the sidecar mounts the Docker socket read-only (/var/run/docker.sock:ro) — a scoped privilege noted as a new stack dependency + attack-surface item for the deploy review'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a scoped privilege' with 'a full Docker API mount (Unix socket :ro does not restrict API calls); the blast radius = full Docker daemon access, scoped only by the autoheal image's own behavior'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is the correct `[capability]` key in Gerrit 3.14.1's `project.config` schema and that it covers both single-change and chain rebase — cite the Gerrit 3.14.1 docs or a live Gerrit instance test in S5b before committing the `setup-all-projects.sh` stanza.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a sufficient, scoped Gerrit server-level capability for the autolander bot — 'a scoped, minimum-necessary grant, NOT broad Impersonate'. This is an unverified capability claim specific to Gerrit 3.14.1: the plan does not cite the Gerrit 3.14.1 release notes or docs confirming (a) the exact capability name and stanza syntax, (b) that it covers both single-change `POST /rebase` and `POST /rebase:chain`, and (c) that it is distinct from and does not imply `Impersonate`. The prior art for this capability (Chromium CQ, Zuul) is cited in the parent story but not for this specific Gerrit capability name.
findings.location: S5b AC — 'grants the RebarBotNava service user the rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader server-level capability (All-Projects refs/meta/config [capability]) — a scoped, minimum-necessary grant, NOT broad Impersonate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin a specific `willfarrell/autoheal` image tag (e.g. `v0.9.0`) and verify its Docker Hub manifest includes a `linux/arm64` variant before merging S5a, to prevent a platform-mismatch failure on the t4g Graviton host.
findings.finding: The willfarrell/autoheal image is introduced without pinning a version tag, and its arm64 (linux/arm64) manifest availability is unverified. The EC2 host is confirmed arm64 (t4g.large / Graviton, `aarch64` per compose-up.sh line 63). If the chosen autoheal tag has no arm64 variant, `docker compose up` will fail at pull time with a platform-mismatch error on this host.
findings.location: S5a AC — docker-compose.yml autoheal sidecar image reference
findings.suggested_fix: Add `image: willfarrell/autoheal:v0.9.0` (verify latest tag with arm64 manifest on hub.docker.com/r/willfarrell/autoheal/tags) and optionally add `platform: linux/arm64` to the compose service entry to make the architecture requirement explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the transport security posture of the status endpoint in S5c: either 'HTTP is acceptable because it binds to loopback/Docker-internal only and never traverses a network segment' OR 'HTTPS with a self-signed or internal CA cert is required because it is reachable cross-host.'
findings.finding: The status endpoint has no declared transport security (TLS vs plain HTTP). If it is reachable beyond localhost — which is ambiguous per the 'bound so agents can reach it' language — plaintext HTTP would expose internal operational metadata in transit.
findings.location: S5c AC – 'Read-only status endpoint' / child ticket 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal `depends_on: S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192)` dep link to S5d's ticket so tooling enforces the EDIT-after-NEW ordering for Dockerfile.autolander.
findings.finding: S5d edits `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` (to add HEALTHCHECK) but that file is created (NEW) by S5a, and no formal dependency link exists between S5d and S5a. If S5d is executed before S5a, the EDIT has no base file to modify.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e — Files section / Dockerfile.autolander EDIT
findings.suggested_fix: Add `depends_on: 08e1-5505-d898-4192` to S5d's ticket deps, and note in S5d's Files section that the Dockerfile.autolander EDIT presupposes S5a's NEW.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Establish a declared ordering between S5c and S5d for loop.py (e.g. S5d merges first as it writes the heartbeat file S5c reads), and specify in both children how the endpoint handles the heartbeat file being absent on first startup.
findings.finding: Both S5c and S5d edit `infra/autolander/loop.py` with no declared ordering, coordination mechanism, or merge sequencing between them, creating an implicit shared-state / ordering gap for the single most critical file in the service.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 + child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e — Files sections (loop.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Declare S5c depends_on S5d (so heartbeat write exists before the endpoint serves heartbeat_age_s). Add a startup guard in S5c's endpoint: if the heartbeat file is absent, return heartbeat_age_s: null rather than erroring, so land's lander_down degrades gracefully.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ownership (to S5a or S5b) for adding the `env_file: .env` (or equivalent `environment:` block) to the autolander service in docker-compose.yml, and add it as an explicit AC or file note in that child.
findings.finding: No child specifies how `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` (written into the shared `.env` by S5b's fetch-secrets.sh) is injected into the autolander Docker container's environment. The `docker-compose.yml` edit owned by S5a does not mention an `env_file:` or `environment:` directive, creating an owned-by-none gap.
findings.location: child ea61-01c3-daea-4824 (S5b) + child 08e1-5505-d898-4192 (S5a) — docker-compose.yml EDIT
findings.suggested_fix: In S5a's docker-compose.yml EDIT section, add: 'the autolander service declares `env_file: [.env]` so fetch-secrets.sh's output reaches the container runtime.' Mirror this as a test step: confirm AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN is set inside the running container.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit `depends_on: S5a, S5d` links to S5b's ticket (or split the smoke test into a separate integration task) so the container-based credential test has a gated pre-condition.
findings.finding: S5b's smoke-test AC ('bot can authenticate + submit a test change on the instance') implicitly requires a running, HEALTHCHECK-passing container, which is only fully assembled after S5a (compose service) and S5d (HEALTHCHECK) are complete — but S5b declares no dependency on either child, creating an ordering violation for the integration test.
findings.location: child ea61-01c3-daea-4824 (S5b) — Tests AC
findings.suggested_fix: Refactor S5b's Tests AC into two parts: (1) unit-verifiable: 'fetch-secrets.sh maps the token into .env' (testable without a container); (2) integration: 'running container authenticates to Gerrit and submits a test change' — mark the integration part as gated on S5a+S5d completion.

findings.finding: No proving command verifies that the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` capability and submit permission are actually effective for `autolander-bot` — the verification section only checks 'authenticates (SSM token → .env → HTTP)', which passes even if the Gerrit account lacks the required capability.
findings.location: AC8 (Verification) — 'confirms it authenticates (SSM token → .env → HTTP)'

findings.finding: AC7 defines a time-bounded drain: if the in-flight wipChain is still blocked on CI past the SIGTERM deadline, the service must record state and exit anyway. AC8 only tests the happy-path drain ('drain-on-shutdown records the in-flight outcome') and never tests the timeout/forced-exit branch, leaving the deadline logic unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8 (Verification/tests), cross-referenced with AC7 (Emergency-stop + drain)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC8, add: 'Drain-timeout test: inject a mock wipChain that never completes; send SIGTERM; assert the process exits within (deadline + 5 s); assert the S3/state outcome marker is written before exit. Record exit time and marker presence in the DD entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the form and repo path of the 'concrete recovery record' in the S5d AC (e.g., 'a `docs/autolander-recovery.md` runbook section covering drain, crash restart, and sentinel-stop procedures').
findings.finding: The 'concrete recovery record' deliverable in S5d's AC is undefined as to artifact type and format — an executor cannot determine what constitutes completion (a runbook file, a YAML doc, a README section, an inline code comment, or something else).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5d bullet: 'drain-on-SIGTERM with a concrete recovery record'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'with a concrete recovery record' with 'with a recovery runbook committed to `docs/autolander-recovery.md` covering: (a) normal SIGTERM drain sequence, (b) crash-restart procedure via autoheal, (c) emergency-stop sentinel activation and reset steps.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the autoheal restart SLA in the plan (e.g., 'autoheal restart delay ≤ 10 s, so the container is back before the 90 s lander_down alarm fires') to make the S5a AC measurable and consistent with the heartbeat contract.
findings.finding: The autoheal restart SLA is not stated — the plan requires 'crash-safe restart' and 'autoheal sidecar' but gives no restart time bound or retry policy, leaving the executor to pick an arbitrary default with no measurable pass/fail criterion.
findings.location: Design context — 'Reliability posture' bullet; S5a AC — 'autoheal sidecar'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the Heartbeat contract or S5a AC: 'autoheal restart delay: 10 s (ensures container restarts well within the 90 s lander_down threshold).'
This also doubles as a test oracle: `docker kill autolander && sleep 20 && curl /status | jq .heartbeat_age_s` should return < 90.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence in the Design context (or S5d AC) that defines S3's 'outcome marker' artifact (file path, format, semantics) and explains exactly how the drain-on-SIGTERM logic in S5d reads or clears it for crash-safe reconciliation.
findings.finding: The Dependencies section states S5 'Uses S3's outcome marker for drain/restart reconciliation,' but neither the Design context, Scope, nor S5d's AC explains what S3's 'outcome marker' is or how it mechanically feeds into drain/restart reconciliation — leaving an unresolved cross-section gap between the declared dependency and the liveness/recovery design.
findings.location: Dependencies section ('Uses S3's outcome marker for drain/restart reconciliation') vs. Design context and S5d AC ('drain-on-SIGTERM with a concrete recovery record')
findings.suggested_fix: In Design context, add: 'S3's outcome marker is a file at `<state-volume-path>/drain.sentinel` (written by S3 on graceful stop); S5d's drain handler checks for its presence before confirming safe shutdown and removes it on clean restart.'  (Exact path/format should match S3's actual spec; the point is to make the contract explicit here.)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure for the `All-Projects` ACL change in S5b (e.g., 'revert the `All-Projects` meta-config commit via `git revert` on the All-Projects repo' or an inverse `setup-all-projects.sh --remove` invocation) so the step is reversible, not merely acknowledged.
findings.finding: The plan modifies the global Gerrit `All-Projects` ACL (adding `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` capability and submit ACL via `setup-all-projects.sh`) — a change that affects all projects on the Gerrit instance — without providing an explicit rollback or back-out plan. Acknowledgement of the step is present, but the rubric requires a reversibility mechanism, not just acknowledgement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5b (`ea61-01c3-daea-4824`): 'rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader capability + submit ACL via setup-all-projects.sh'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5b's AC or design notes, document: (1) the exact ACL entries being added, (2) the before/after state of the `All-Projects` `project.config`, and (3) the revert command (`git -C All-Projects revert <sha>` + `gerrit flush-caches`) or an idempotent `setup-all-projects.sh --remove` flag that undoes the grant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make heartbeat thresholds (poll interval, freshness window, lander_down age) configurable via environment variables or a config file rather than hardcoded constants, and document the defaults in the child story (S5d) or a config reference.
findings.finding: The heartbeat contract thresholds (15 s poll interval, ≤ 15 s freshness window, > 90 s lander_down trigger) are labeled 'design-fixed,' implying they will be hardcoded constants rather than configurable values. These are operational tuning parameters that site operators are likely to need to adjust (e.g., slower infra, tighter SLO) without a full rebuild/redeploy of the image.
findings.location: Design context — 'Heartbeat contract (design-fixed)' section
findings.suggested_fix: In S5d (and the loop.py hook), read HEARTBEAT_POLL_S (default 15), HEARTBEAT_FRESHNESS_S (default 15), and LANDER_DOWN_THRESHOLD_S (default 90) from environment variables; document them in the compose service definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit deliverable (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update) capturing the single-writer + heartbeat + autoheal architectural decision, the rationale for deferring HA, and the contracts (`heartbeat_age_s`, sentinel file path, flock scope) that downstream consumers depend on.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel reliability pattern for a single-writer bot (heartbeat file + autoheal sidecar + emergency-stop sentinel + drain-on-SIGTERM + flock guard) that has no precedent in the existing codebase, yet no ADR or CLAUDE.md update is called for to capture this architectural decision durably.
findings.location: Design context section + Scope section + AC bullet for S5a/S5d
findings.suggested_fix: Add a child task or AC line: 'Update `docs/adr/NNN-autolander-reliability.md` (or `CLAUDE.md`) with: (a) single-writer rationale, (b) heartbeat contract fields and thresholds, (c) sentinel file location/semantics, (d) HA deferral rationale.' Reference it from the S5 story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly designate the emergency-stop sentinel as the primary rollback mechanism in S5d, specify its effect on in-flight changes (e.g., drain completes vs. abandons), add a rollback runbook reference to the recovery record, and include a rollback scenario in the crash-recovery/idempotency test.
findings.finding: The plan provides no explicit, tested rollback procedure. The emergency-stop sentinel (S5d) is the de-facto undo mechanism, but it is not named as the rollback path, its effect on in-flight changes is unspecified, and no rollback test is called for alongside the crash-recovery/idempotency test.
findings.location: AC bullet for S5d — 'emergency-stop sentinel, drain-on-SIGTERM with a concrete recovery record, and the crash-recovery/idempotency test'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5d AC, add: 'The recovery record includes a rollback runbook: (1) touch <sentinel-path> to halt the loop after the current poll, (2) verify no Autosubmit labels are left in a transitional state, (3) docker-compose stop autolander. A test asserts that sentinel activation causes clean exit with no partial label writes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the autoheal sidecar's Docker socket mount from `:ro` to `:rw` (or remove the `:ro` qualifier), and document the accepted privilege surface in the compose service spec; a read-only mount prevents `docker restart` and defeats hang-recovery.
findings.finding: The willfarrell/autoheal sidecar is specified with `/var/run/docker.sock:ro` (read-only mount), but autoheal issues `docker restart` against the daemon socket — a POST /containers/{id}/restart call that requires write access. A read-only Docker socket mount will silently block the restart call, leaving a hung unhealthy container unrecovered — the exact hang-recovery guarantee the plan is built around.
findings.location: S5a AC (autoheal sidecar) + S5d AC4 (Liveness/restart) — docker-compose.yml willfarrell/autoheal definition
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml change `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro` to `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (rw, default). Add a security note in the service spec acknowledging this grants the sidecar full daemon API access, and restrict the service's network to none to limit lateral blast radius.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the correct Gerrit ACL section for `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader`: confirm whether it is a ref-level `rebase` permission under `[access "refs/heads/*"]` in `All-Projects` (not a `[capability]` entry), and update `setup-all-projects.sh` accordingly — a misplaced grant is silently ignored and will cause every rebase call to 403 at runtime.
findings.finding: The plan grants `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` as a server-level `[capability]` block entry in `All-Projects refs/meta/config`. In Gerrit's ACL model, `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a ref-level PERMISSION (granted under `[access "refs/heads/*"]` as `rebase = group <group>`) — it is NOT a `[capability]` section entry. The `[capability]` section holds site-wide administrative capabilities (`administrateServer`, `batchChangesLimit`, `priority`, `queryLimit`, etc.). Placing it in `[capability]` will result in it being silently ignored by Gerrit, and every POST /changes/{id}/rebase will 403 with insufficient permissions.
findings.location: S5b AC (Capability) — infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh / All-Projects refs/meta/config [capability]
findings.suggested_fix: In the All-Projects refs/meta/config pushed by setup-all-projects.sh, add the grant under [access "refs/heads/*"] as `rebase = group <autolander-service-group>` rather than under [capability]. Cross-reference Gerrit 3.14.1 docs on 'Rebase Changes on Behalf of Uploader' for the exact permission key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure-handling contract for the `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` call in observability.sh: document the expected exit code and output when the autolander container is absent or Docker is unreachable, and apply the same `|| true` + output-guard pattern used by the existing feeders.
findings.finding: The S5c error feeder for `autolander_errors` uses `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` as a new Docker CLI surface in observability.sh. The existing observability.sh pattern for all other error feeders (voter_errors, deploy_errors, g2p_dispatch_errors) uses journalctl log-grep with offset files. The `docker inspect` approach is a different integration surface not currently used in observability.sh and its output format / exit behavior under edge cases (container absent, daemon unreachable) is unspecified in the plan.
findings.location: S5c AC (Error metric — LOUD by default) — infra/scripts/observability.sh autolander_errors feeder
findings.suggested_fix: health_status=$(docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' compose-autolander-1 2>/dev/null || true); [ "$health_status" = "unhealthy" ] && unhealthy_count=1 || unhealthy_count=0 — then publish unhealthy_count as a component of the autolander_errors metric, consistent with the existing feeder pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the `.env` file permissions and confirm each container service only receives its own token (e.g., via Docker `env_file` scoping, per-service env injection, or separate `.env.autolander` / `.env.reviewbot` files) in S5b's AC.
findings.finding: The shared `.env` file that co-locates `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` and `GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN` (review-bot) has no declared file-permission or container-isolation constraint, meaning both tokens are potentially readable by any container that mounts the shared env or any process with host filesystem access.
findings.location: S5b AC / child ticket ea61-01c3-daea-4824 – 'fetch-secrets.sh … materialized into the SAME shared .env'
findings.suggested_fix: Either split into per-service env files (`.env.autolander` with only `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN`) or explicitly state that the shared `.env` is `0600` owned by the deploy user and that each service's `env_file:` stanza in `docker-compose.yml` is scoped to its own file — whichever pattern matches the existing stack convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the parent story's `file_impact` entry from `infra/gerrit/setup-project.sh` to `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` to match S5b's declared file.
findings.finding: The parent story's `file_impact` metadata names `infra/gerrit/setup-project.sh` as the file for All-Projects capability provisioning, but S5b creates a distinct new file `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`. This is a residual reference — the parent's file_impact points to the wrong file path.
findings.location: parent story file_impact vs. child ea61-01c3-daea-4824 (S5b) — Files section
findings.suggested_fix: Change the parent file_impact path to `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` with reason 'NEW: All-Projects capability + submit ACL provisioning (setup-all-projects.sh, not setup-project.sh)'.

findings.finding: The drain-on-SIGTERM description in AC7 conflates two distinct drain outcomes under the same 'write the S3 outcome marker' language. For a wipChain that COMPLETES within the drain window, writing the S3 outcome marker is correct. But for a wipChain still blocked on CI when the deadline expires, the plan says 'record its state and exit anyway' without specifying what format or file that state record takes. If the restarted instance interprets the absence of a recovery record as 'no in-flight work', it will drop the partially-processed stack and re-select from the queue — potentially re-driving a stack that already had its Autosubmit label removed or its tested SHAs reset. The plan leaves this recovery contract underspecified, creating a hidden ordering assumption that the restart instance reads the wipChain recovery file correctly.
findings.location: AC7 (Emergency-stop + drain) — 'if the in-flight wipChain is still blocked on CI past the deadline, record its state and exit anyway rather than blocking the redeploy indefinitely'

findings.finding: The provisioning path for the `All-Projects` `[capability]` grant is a fork ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`'), deferring a real file/location decision to the executor.
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/gerrit/ provisioning' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` (not an extension of the rebar-scoped script), include the exact `[capability]` stanza in the plan, and add an AC proving command: `ssh -p 29418 gerrit gerrit ls-members --recursive 'Service Users' | grep autolander-bot && git archive --remote=ssh://gerrit:29418/All-Projects refs/meta/config project.config | tar -xO | grep -A2 rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader`.

findings.finding: The Scope section explicitly states that AC5 (heartbeat write), AC6 (queue-status endpoint + time-to-land metric emission), and AC7 (emergency-stop sentinel check, drain-on-shutdown) 'live in the same service module (`infra/autolander/loop.py`)' — but `infra/autolander/loop.py` does not appear anywhere in the Files section (neither as a new file nor as an edited file). This is a direct cross-section contradiction between Scope and Files, and an E1 orphan: three ACs describe deliverables in a specific named file that the implementation file list omits entirely.
findings.location: Scope section ('live in the same service module (`infra/autolander/loop.py`)') vs Files section (no entry for loop.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Under Files > Existing files edited, add: `infra/autolander/loop.py` — add heartbeat timestamp write on each poll iteration (AC5), stat the emergency-stop sentinel each iteration and transition to `paused` phase (AC7), emit time-to-land `PutMetricData` on successful submit (AC6); and if the HTTP status server is a separate module, add `infra/autolander/status_server.py` (new) serving the AC6 JSON endpoint.

findings.finding: AC6 specifies the bot emits a 'raw per-landed-change queue-wait/resident-time duration' via `PutMetricData` as individual `Value` datapoints, but the metric name (the exact `--metric-name` string) is never stated anywhere in the plan. Every other metric defined in this plan and codebase has an explicit name (`autolander_errors`, `voter_errors`, `replication_errors`, etc.). Without the name, the `monitoring_autolander.tf` alarm reference and any `observability.sh` feeder cannot be consistently wired across files.
findings.location: AC6 (Queue-status + liveness surface) — Time-to-land metric paragraph

findings.finding: The Files section leaves the Gerrit provisioning file undetermined ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`'), while AC2 treats the All-Projects capability grant as a concrete deliverable that must be verified. This creates a cross-section gap: AC2 specifies what must be provisioned but the Files section does not commit to which file delivers it.
findings.location: Files section ('infra/gerrit/ provisioning') vs AC2

findings.finding: The HTTP status endpoint (AC6) — a new user-facing API whose `heartbeat_age_s` field S4's `lander_down` directly binds to — has no test coverage in AC8 whatsoever: no happy-path assertion, no schema validation, and no test of the empty/startup state (e.g., no wipChain yet). If the field name is wrong or the JSON shape diverges from the contract, the test suite goes green while S4 silently misreads liveness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8 (Verification/tests), cross-referenced with AC6 (Queue-status endpoint)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC8, add: 'The live boot test curls the status endpoint (`curl -s http://localhost:<port>/status`) and asserts: HTTP 200, JSON body contains `heartbeat_age_s` (integer), `wip_chain` (null or object), and `autosubmit_waiting` (integer). Record the curl output in the DD entry as observable proof.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `setup-project.sh` manages 'only the `rebar` project's `refs/meta/config`' (verified true), then concludes the `All-Projects` `[capability]` grant for `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` needs 'a named provisioning step (extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`)'. However, neither option is decided — it is left as 'exact... provisioning path is the operator-cutover surface'. No concrete file is added to the Files list for this step. The scope exclusion 'the exact stanza and provisioning path is the operator-cutover surface' is used to DELETE the work from the plan's deliverables, but a false exclusion here means the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` grant has no automated provisioning path at all, leaving the bot unable to rebase on behalf of uploaders on the live server.
findings.location: Files section / infra/gerrit/ provisioning bullet (All-Projects [capability] grant)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` to the Files section as a new file that uses `git fetch/checkout refs/meta/config` on `All-Projects`, appends `[capability]\n\trebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users` if absent, and pushes — following the same clone→edit→push pattern as `setup-project.sh`. Register it in the operator cutover runbook.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a server-level capability granted in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` under `[capability]`, but no provisioning script or config snippet for this grant is specified, and there is zero prior use of `[capability]` blocks in the repo's `infra/gerrit/` scripts. The `setup-project.sh` only manages the `rebar` project's `refs/meta/config`, not `All-Projects`. This is a new integration surface — Unverified at the config-syntax and provisioning-path level — and a capability gap: the plan names the requirement but does not name which script adds it or provide the exact `[capability]` / `[access]` stanza.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC2 (Credentials mechanism)

findings.finding: The All-Projects provisioning step has an unresolved forked edit target: the plan says 'extend setup-project.sh or add infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh' without committing to either, so no single file is definitively in the edit set. Neither infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh (does not exist) nor a concrete decision to extend setup-project.sh is made. This means the implementer has no designated file to edit or create.
findings.location: Files section — infra/gerrit/ provisioning paragraph

findings.finding: The Gerrit provisioning path for the All-Projects capability grant is an unresolved OR: 'extend setup-project.sh or add infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh'. An executor must make a design choice that is architectural (one file vs. a new file with its own invocation wiring).
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/gerrit/ provisioning'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
